Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed verb phrase functioning as an intransitive or transitive predicate; often appears after modals like 'like' or aspect markers like 'stay.'
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To chat casually, share news, build connection, or pass time through informal conversation.
Usage Contexts: Daily speech; family gatherings; workplace breaks; community events; friendly introductions.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Common across Hawaiʻi’s plantation-era multilingual communities, where storytelling and casual conversation helped people from different language backgrounds form social ties.
First Example Sentence: We was sitting outside da store, just talk story till da rain pau.
Second Example Sentence: Aunty like talk story wit you aftah church, so no run away fast.
Third Example Sentence: Every Friday, da crew stay talk story by da truck before clocking in.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Adjectival or adverbial modifier; flexible quantifier placed before nouns, adjectives, or clauses.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Somewhat, a little, minor, or not a big deal; can soften a statement or reduce intensity.
Usage Contexts: Everyday description; requests; complaints; humor; cautious opinions.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects local speech habits of indirectness and moderation, often used to avoid sounding too blunt or dramatic.
First Example Sentence: I small kine tired, but I still can help you move da cooler.
Second Example Sentence: Da meeting was small kine long, but da food was ono.
Third Example Sentence: He wen get small kine shame when everybody clapped for him.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Pronoun-like placeholder noun phrase; deictic expression used when a word is unknown, obvious, sensitive, or unnecessary.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A flexible substitute for a person, thing, action, quality, or idea understood from context.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: That thing; that person; the unspecified item or idea.
Usage Contexts: Daily speech; repairs; cooking; directions; teasing; shared-context conversation.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Developed in Hawaiʻi Creole English as a versatile discourse tool, especially useful in multilingual settings where speakers relied on context, gesture, and shared knowledge.
First Example Sentence: Pass me da kine on top da counter, da one next to da rice cooker.
Second Example Sentence: I went call da kine already, so da appointment stay set for Monday.
Third Example Sentence: She no like say da name, but everybody knew who da kine was.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Noun phrase used predicatively or as an object; metaphorical body-state expression.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A strong physical feeling of awe, fear, emotion, or spiritual intensity, like goosebumps.
Usage Contexts: Ghost stories; music; sports; family memories; spiritual or emotional moments.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Connected to local storytelling traditions, including spooky night stories, music performances, and deeply felt communal experiences.
First Example Sentence: When da kūpuna started singing, I got chicken skin all ova my arms.
Second Example Sentence: Dat ghost story gave everybody chicken skin, even da uncle who neva get scared.
Third Example Sentence: I got chicken skin when da boy scored da last touchdown for Waianae.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed noun phrase and time expression; can function adverbially after work-related clauses.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: The time after work, or the relaxed social mood that begins when the workday is finished.
Usage Contexts: Workplace; social plans; food and drink meetups; scheduling; local business culture.
Register and Tone: Colloquial to semi-formal, friendly, locally professional.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: From Hawaiian 'pau' meaning finished and 'hana' meaning work; widely used in Hawaiʻi workplaces and social life.
First Example Sentence: Meet us pau hana by da beach park, and bring one chair if you get.
Second Example Sentence: Boss said we can clean up early, so everybody wen smile for pau hana.
Third Example Sentence: Da traffic stay nuts during pau hana, so I going leave town little bit late.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed imperative or exclamatory phrase; borrowed Hawaiian expression used as a discourse marker.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Do it again; encore; repeat something enjoyable or successful.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Again; one more time; repeat it.
Usage Contexts: Music; hula; performances; sports; classroom encouragement; playful requests.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: From Hawaiian 'hana hou' meaning 'do again'; common at performances and community events across Hawaiʻi.
First Example Sentence: Da band played so good, da whole crowd yelled hana hou.
Second Example Sentence: Coach told da kids hana hou aftah dey finally ran da drill right.
Third Example Sentence: Your laulau came out ono, so next family party gotta be hana hou.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Modal negative predicate; compact fixed clause replacing 'cannot' or 'is not possible.'
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Something cannot be done, is not allowed, or is not possible under the circumstances.
Usage Contexts: Commands; refusals; rules; logistics; practical problem-solving.
Register and Tone: Colloquial, direct, efficient; can sound firm or matter-of-fact.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects Hawaiian Pidgin’s streamlined grammar shaped by contact among English, Hawaiian, Cantonese, Portuguese, Japanese, Filipino languages, and others.
First Example Sentence: No can park ova dea, da tow truck come quick.
Second Example Sentence: I like help you today, but no can because I get doctor appointment.
Third Example Sentence: Da gate stay locked already, so no can go inside till morning.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Verb phrase with light verb 'make' plus compound object; transitive or intransitive depending on context.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: To glare at someone with disapproval, suspicion, anger, or silent warning.
Usage Contexts: Family scolding; school; workplace tension; humor; conflict avoidance.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Local expression tied to nonverbal communication norms, where facial expression can carry strong social meaning without direct confrontation.
First Example Sentence: Uncle wen make stink eye when da boys tracked mud inside da house.
Second Example Sentence: She no say nothing, but she made stink eye from across da room.
Third Example Sentence: Da cashier made stink eye because da braddah cut da line.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Verb phrase with transitive construction; hyperbolic evaluative idiom, usually for food.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Extremely delicious, so good it overwhelms the eater.
Usage Contexts: Food reviews; family cooking; plate lunch talk; markets; celebrations.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Rooted in Hawaiʻi’s food culture, where local identity is strongly expressed through shared meals, plate lunches, and multicultural cooking traditions.
First Example Sentence: Dat garlic chicken broke da mouth; I had to go back for one second plate.
Second Example Sentence: Grandma’s pork adobo stay broke da mouth every time she cook um slow.
Third Example Sentence: Da mango from their yard was so sweet, broke da mouth fo real.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Adjectival predicate phrase with intensifier 'all' and participial adjective 'buss up.'
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Badly damaged, messy, worn out, emotionally shaken, or physically exhausted.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Very damaged; very messy; worn out.
Usage Contexts: Car trouble; sports injuries; messy rooms; emotional stress; joking complaints.
Register and Tone: Colloquial, vivid, mildly rough but not vulgar.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Likely influenced by English 'burst/bust' and local phonology; common in Hawaiʻi talk about objects, bodies, and situations that are in bad shape.
First Example Sentence: My slippers stay all buss up from walking ova da lava rocks.
Second Example Sentence: Aftah da double shift, everybody looked all buss up but still laughing.
Third Example Sentence: Da old truck was all buss up, but somehow it still made it up da pali.
